hero.title: How to use FoldBridge
hero.summary: A practical guide to navigating the portal: finding entries, reading the master table, exploring a detail page, and interpreting the confidence labels. For the science behind the data and how confidence is computed, see the About page.
hero.detail: This page answers “how do I use this site”. If you want the methodology — data sources, how structural truth is derived, and how tiers are earned — the About page carries the full write-up.

items.body: The master table of structure-linked PDB entries. Start here to browse, filter and search all published entries, then open any row to its detail page.
The science library: explainer articles on each probing technology, grouped into six mechanism families, plus a 34-technology comparison table and a glossary.
A build-time snapshot of what the database links — entry counts, probe technologies, RNA biology coverage and how confidence distributes across recall tiers.
Site-wide search across probing articles and PDB cases. Use it when you know a molecule, PDB id or technology name.
What FoldBridge is and the full methodology: data sources, how the structural truth (paired state / SASA / contacts) is computed, and how confidence is calibrated.

Each row summarises a set of published PDB chains that share the same biological molecule within one structure. When a PDB has both RMDB and RASP sources, they are merged into one row while links back to each source case are preserved.

A case-level summary of measurement families (A–F) and recall tiers present across the entry's chains — not a single best-profile score. Family C is an exploratory hint and should be re-checked against the route assets.
Narrow the table by the available filters and group by year or category. Only structures with generated detail-page assets are listed — the published allowlist.
A RASP entry marked not active has no scorable reactivity signal. This is a property of the data, not a negative confidence result.
Pick which reactivity profile to view. Profiles are grouped by measurement family; profiles without a family appear under “Unassigned family” so every loaded profile stays selectable.
Per-residue tracks aligned to the PDB chain: profile position, sequence, PDB alignment, structure state, coordinates, targetability, reactivity, DBN bridge and interaction endpoints.
A VARNA diagram of the chain, colored by the same reactivity scale as the 1D and 3D views. The view auto-fits the whole molecule.
A Mol* viewer of the target chain, colored by reactivity. Click a residue in the 1D or 2D view to focus the 3D camera on it; hovering the 3D structure highlights the matching 1D / 2D position.
The 1D, 2D and 3D panels are linked: selecting a residue in one highlights it in the others, so the same nucleotide is traceable across all three representations.

sections.title: Reading a confidence label at a glance
sections.body: Every confidence label is two words: a measurement family (A–F) and a calibrated recall tier. The family tells you which physical quantity the experiment measured — it is not a quality grade, and A is not “better” than D. The tier tells you how reliably that evidence recovers the deposited structure after a permutation test. Read it as family + tier: all of the strength lives in the tier. STRONG and MODERATE are supported; WEAK / DISCORDANT / UNDERPOWERED / NOT_SUPPORTED each flag a specific limitation. The About page has the full definitions of every family and tier.

sections.title: Recall tiers, in one line each

items.body: Permutation-calibrated and passing every gate — the evidence strongly supports the structure.
Passes the relaxed gates — the evidence supports the structure reasonably well (MODERATE_CANDIDATE when uncalibrated).
The statistic clears its bar but a secondary gate (self-containment / conflict / sample size) does not.
The probing signal disagrees in direction with the structural truth and needs review.
Too little data to draw a conclusion (fewer than 15 evaluable, or under 5 paired / unpaired residues).
Not significant under the permutation test — a high-looking score no better than chance once labels are shuffled.

items.term: Why is the 3D view mostly one color?
items.body: The default profile may be sparse. Use the profile selector to pick a richer profile; residues without reactivity data are shown in neutral grey by design (no color extrapolation).

items.term: Why does an entry show no STRONG tier?
items.body: STRONG requires a passed permutation test plus quality gates. Most entries honestly do not reach it; the tier reflects the evidence, not a site limitation.

items.term: Where do the structures load from?
items.body: Tertiary structures are fetched from the RCSB PDB at view time; the portal itself stores the probing signal, alignments and confidence, not the full coordinate files.

items.term: A threshold looks arbitrary — is it published?
items.body: Often not. Only RL-Seq's Spearman cut-points are literature-set; most thresholds are operating values pending calibration, labelled truthfully in the registry. See the About page's threshold-honesty statement.
